problem_impact:
Over the past decade, children have been unknowingly placed in a vast, unregulated social experiment. They have been exposed to social media algorithms and online gaming communities without the necessary guidance or safeguards. This has led to measurable negative effects on their well-being, including increased anxiety, reduced attention spans, poor sleep, and social withdrawal.

In response, many adults have begun to restrict children's screen time. However, this approach focuses too much on quantity and not enough on quality. Not all screen time is created equal. Creative, social, and guided digital play can offer powerful developmental benefits, whereas passive or addictive content consumption does not.

By failing to distinguish between harmful and beneficial digital experiences, we risk throwing out the good with the bad and further isolating children and missing an opportunity to build healthier, more empowering relationships with technology.
customer_type: {Parents/Guardians/Carers}
elevator_pitch: By helping children to gain & maintain new friendships, to explore online networks safely and to learn valuable life skills through play, we transform screen time into quality time in our weekly, guided Minecraft clubs.
solution_explanation: We transform screen time into quality time by embedding social-emotional learning into safe, guided online gaming experiences. Children join live, story-driven Minecraft clubs led by trained game educators (gedus), where they build friendships, practice communication, and develop real-life skills through play. Instead of isolating or passive screen use, our model turns gaming into a meaningful, communal, and growth-oriented experience.
program_goals: We want to use AI to scale our game-based learning platform globally without losing the human touch. We believe that this program can help us in our work to develop AI tools that enhance learning, support digital youth work, and help us bring safe, playful, and pedagogically sound gaming experiences to millions of children worldwide.

ai_problem_solving:
At School of Gaming, we use AI to:

1. Personalize learning experiences for each child in real time, adapting narratives, challenges, and support based on their behavior and skill level inside the game world (e.g., Minecraft).

2. Support game educators (gedus) with AI copilots that help them plan lessons, manage group dynamics, and reflect on learning outcomes, even across language and cultural boundaries.

3. Build safe and meaningful digital environments, where AI can detect toxic behavior, mediate conflicts, and reinforce positive social interaction through in-game agents.

4. Create narrative and content at scale, allowing us to maintain strong storytelling and motivational elements across thousands of sessions and multiple countries without losing the pedagogical depth.

elevator_pitch: BuzzGage helps schools measure what matters beyond academics.
solution_explanation:
BuzzGage solves the problem by providing school leaders with a comprehensive, AI-powered student development pointers benchmarked against international standards. The system begins by collecting student feedback through short, research-backed sessions twice a year. These sessions measure multiple areas of student wellbeing, engagement, and development across cognitive, behavioral, emotional, and social domains.

Once the data is collected, BuzzGage uses AI to analyze patterns, identify early signs of disengagement, and benchmark results against international education frameworks such as CASEL, OECD, and the IB Learner Profile. This allows schools to see not just how students feel and function, but how they compare globally on key whole-child indicators.

The platform then generates tailored insights and actionable strategies for each school based on its specific context. These insights are presented through clear dashboards, enabling leadership teams to prioritize areas of concern and implement targeted programs for student support. 

By closing the loop between measurement and action, BuzzGage ensures that student voice becomes a driver of whole-school improvement.

We’re currently working with publicly funded schools in the UK and hope to deepen our presence in this space. Through this programme, we aim to gain expert guidance on scaling AI-driven education tools, validating our impact model, and expanding our reach in the UK and globally. We also hope to connect with mentors and partners who can support this journey.

problem_description: In South Africa, most learners leave primary school without mastering basic numeracy. By Grade 4, many struggle with fractions, place value, and multiplication, critical foundations for later learning. Large class sizes (often 40+ learners), language barriers, and limited parental support mean teachers cannot provide personalised feedback. Homework is often left incomplete, copied, or unsupported. This learning gap compounds over time, leading to low confidence and poor maths outcomes. Vumba tackles this early, ensuring every child has access to practice, feedback, and support, no matter their context.

problem_impact: Grade 4 is a make-or-break year in South Africa, when learners transition from learning to read to reading to learn, and from simple counting to abstract maths. When learners don’t master basics like fractions or place value, they quickly fall behind. Without support, many lose confidence, disengage from maths, and stop completing homework. These gaps compound into later grades, contributing to high dropout rates by Grade 9, when mathematics becomes a gatekeeper for further study and career options.

elevator_pitch: Zuzu Maths is a WhatsApp-based  maths  homework tool for primary learners. It gives instant feedback, spots mistakes, and offers support like a smart tutor. Teachers get clear reports to guide their lessons and better support each child in class.
solution_explanation:
Many learners in South Africa struggle with maths homework because they do not get timely help at home. Teachers are often too overwhelmed to give each child personal feedback. This leads to repeated mistakes, frustration, and a drop in confidence.

Vumba is a WhatsApp-based homework assistant that helps Grade 4 learners practise maths every day. Learners receive short, curriculum-aligned missions on WhatsApp, complete practice questions, and get instant feedback. If they make a mistake, Vumba’s AI identifies the misconception and gives a helpful hint or explanation, just like a good tutor would.

Vumba does not replace the teacher. It supports them. Teachers receive summary reports showing what learners found difficult so they can adjust their lessons. Parents also get simple updates to help them stay involved.

By turning daily homework into an interactive and personalised experience, Vumba helps learners build confidence, practise more effectively, and make progress, even in large and under-resourced classrooms.
program_goals: We want to sharpen Vumba into a truly scalable and sustainable solution. We’ve built strong foundations with our misconception-tagging machine learning model, learner motivation and rewards, and teacher support features. Now we need to strengthen our revenue model, improve how we measure success, and build the right team to grow. This programme can help us refine our strategy, ground our work in solid research, and prepare to scale across and beyond South Africa.

ai_problem_solving: AI allows Vumba to provide personalised feedback and diagnose misconceptions at scale, something teachers in overcrowded classrooms simply cannot do. With more than 40 learners per class, it is nearly impossible for a teacher to catch every error pattern or adapt practice for each child. AI makes this possible by classifying mistakes in real time, generating tailored hints, and recommending follow-up tasks. Without AI, learners would continue receiving generic worksheets and delayed feedback, leaving gaps unaddressed and confidence diminished.

He has worked on AI models for classification and prediction. Heaven holds a master’s degree in Digital Business and Innovation from IE Business School, where he also participated in AI hackathons and co-developed an R programming course for postgraduate students at the University of Kassel in Germany. Together, we apply AI in practical, learner-focused ways to solve real classroom challenges.

problem_impact:
EYESmart was founded by Shukun and Yan, childcare professionals with over a decade of hands-on nursery management experience. Both hold MAs in Early Years and have lived the challenges that nurseries and childminders face daily — from endless paperwork to regulatory pressure and the need to keep families engaged.
They created EYESmart because they saw first-hand how much valuable time educators were losing to repetitive paperwork, how inconsistent and subjective observations could be, and how difficult it was for parents to feel truly engaged in their child’s development. They knew that if technology could:
Save time,
Deliver more consistent and accurate observations,
Spot developmental or safeguarding issues earlier, and
Empower parents with meaningful insights and next steps —
then nurseries, childminders, and families would all benefit.
EYESmart was born out of this lived experience: a platform built by practitioners, for practitioners, combining deep early years expertise with innovative AI technology. This professional background provides the credibility and trust that nurseries and parents are looking for in a new solution.

EYESmart helps nurseries, childminders, and parents capture, assess, and celebrate every stage of a child’s development — with AI-powered tools that save time, ensure compliance, and strengthen family–educator partnerships.
solution_explanation:
Our platform uses AI to analyse videos or photos of children’s daily activities and transform them into structured, compliant written observations. Meanwhile, AI links the observations to Early Years Foundation Stages (EYFS) Framework to generate accurate assessment about the child’s development, and flags anomalies aligned with the SEND code of practice and behaviour code spontaneously. On this basis, gives activities suggestions to both parents and teachers for next steps of child’s development. 
EYESmart is the only integrated AI tool for both parents and teachers to capture every single moment during the children’s development journey in early years.

1) Support teachers to create full, curriculum-aligned courses (flashcards, quizzes, and videos)

2) Give every student a personalised StudyPal AI agent that explains topics, answers questions, and adapts practice in real time, 24/7.

3) Generate varied assessments tied to outcomes and provide instant, rubric-aware feedback so students learn from every attempt while teachers save marking time.

problem_impact:
The lack of year-over-year IEP management creates real challenges for both families and students:

For Parents & Caregivers
Repetition & Advocacy Fatigue: Each year, parents must re-explain their child’s needs and history to new teachers, leading to frustration and burnout.
Limited Visibility: Without consistent records, parents lack clear evidence of progress or regression, weakening their ability to advocate effectively.
Distrust & Stress: Gaps in communication reduce confidence in the education system and increase stress in managing their child’s learning journey.

For Students
Interrupted Support: Loss of historical data means proven strategies aren’t consistently carried forward, disrupting learning progress.
Delayed Interventions: Missing patterns and trends delays timely adjustments in teaching methods and accommodations.
Emotional & Academic Setbacks: Inconsistency can erode self-confidence, motivation, and lead to widening achievement gaps compared to peers.

elevator_pitch: We help students and institutions future-proof their tech careers in an AI first world.
solution_explanation:
Crayonics is a browser-based career-readiness platform for students and institution that turns classroom outputs into employer-ready evidence. It helps learners build automation-resilient skills via personalised assessments, employer-aligned project work, adaptive interview practice, and verified certification.

Intake & assessment
-Students complete a short questionnaire and upload a CV.

-The system runs a quick parse + baseline Automation Resilience Score (ARS) across three dimensions: Technical Resilience, Transferable Skills, and Career Viability.

Personalised learning pathway
-ARS + questionnaire drive a recommended path: Beginner → Intermediate → Advanced project tracks mapped to curriculum objectives and employer needs.

-Projects are modular and low-lift for teachers (lesson pack + rubric + starter repo).

Hands-on project work
Students complete employer-aligned projects (portfolio artifacts). Projects are designed to emphasise human-centric judgment, collaboration, and AI-augmentation i.e., skills that are difficult to fully automate.

Adaptive mock interviews & feedback
LLM-powered interview simulator generates role-specific, difficulty-adjusted questions and rubriced feedback.
Feedback ties to ARS sub-scores so students see exactly which competencies to improve.

Certification & employer signalling
On verified completion, students receive digital certificates/badges (Sertifier) and a shareable portfolio item employers can validate.

Educator and employer dashboards let reviewers inspect work and see measurable indicators (project score, ARS change, interview rubric results).

Analytics & teacher enablement
-Teacher dashboards provide cohort views, at-risk flags, and suggested lesson integrations to reduce teacher workload.

-Automated reporting turns pilot results into measurable outcomes (pre/post ARS, completion rates, employer feedback).

problem_description:
We are addressing outdated learning tools and deep inequality in access to effective educational support, for young people across the UK (and then beyond).

Private tuition and high-end learning platforms are financially out of reach for many low-income families. The 2023 Sutton Trust report 'Tutoring: The New Landscape' states that only 12.8% of pupils from the lowest quartile of household income receive any private tuition, compared to 31.6% in the highest quartile. We remark that this percentage is about receiving **any** private tuition; it's likely that those with more wealth also receive more tuition overall (e.g. in more subjects). This disparity is a major driver of the attainment gap: pupils from disadvantaged backgrounds are, on average, 19 months behind their peers by the end of secondary school (Education Policy Institute).

At the same time, existing tools fail to engage (and thus help) the modern learner. Young people are growing up with interactive, fast-feedback digital experiences (TikTok, Instagram Reels, video games), yet most educational resources remain static, text-heavy, and unengaging. This leads to disengagement, poor retention, and reduced motivation to practise.

The short-term impact is students underperforming in exams and at school, but the long-term impact is entrenched inequality, reduced career options, and perpetuation of socio-economic divides. Without intervention, these patterns will continue to affect hundreds of thousands of students annually at a national scale.

With regards to the question below ‘What are the key causes of the problem you identify above?’, we have ticked the boxes that we think contribute to why the problem has not yet been solved successfully.

problem_impact:
Taking GCSE Maths as an example, in England, only 59.6% of students achieved a grade 4 or above in 2024, and in the very recent 2025 results, this is even worse (58.2%) (FFT Education Datalab). The consequences are significant: GCSE Maths is a gateway requirement for most further education courses, apprenticeships, and many careers. Failing it can lock students out of higher education and higher-paid roles, contributing to cycles of disadvantage.

Compounding this issue across further subjects means that students who disengage with traditional revision methods risk underperforming more broadly, reinforcing a cycle where lack of motivation and inadequate support leads to multiple poor outcomes. The result is a significant group of young people being left behind, not necessarily because of lack of ability, but because current tools and systems fail to engage and support them in the way that they need.

FFT Education Datalab: https://ffteducationdatalab.org.uk/2025/08/gcse-results-2025-the-main-trends-in-grades-and-entries/

elevator_pitch: GoMaths makes GCSE Maths support affordable and available, with an AI tutor that teaches and motivates. By properly combining AI, education, and game design, we create a learning experience that’s both effective and engaging.
solution_explanation:
With new AI models (particularly conversational models like ChatGPT), the traditional role of a good human tutor is becoming increasingly replicable. We feel that the majority of the core features can now be feasibly emulated (and also exceeded), if executed well.

Adaptive Tutoring:
With GoMaths, each student can follow a personalised pathway through the GCSE Maths syllabus, with our models monitoring for misconceptions and key understanding, directing the student and keeping them challenged, but not overwhelmed. We’ll be able to offer tailored explanations, step-by-step walkthroughs, and scaffolded follow-ups, just like a human tutor.

Importantly, we’re not just a ‘ChatGPT wrapper’; we’ll be tailoring our models specifically to the domain of GCSE Maths. For instance, our curated content bank will be used in tandem with RAG, to ensure high-levels of accuracy and confidence with generated output (something that a generic tool like ChatGPT cannot compete with).

Moreover, with many GCSE Maths students using our platform, we’ll have access to a wealth of data. Through significant analysis of this information, we can keep improving the product by (e.g.) determining which questions/explanations/walkthroughs are working the best, and which pathways are optimal for engagement.

We plan for our first point of implementation to be in the design of discrete ‘concept walkthroughs’, that teach a difficult maths concept end-to-end. By modelling the student’s progress in something akin to state space, we aim for high adaptability and success in helping a student really understand something.

Motivational Design:
We intend for gamification to be a core feature within GoMaths, as it’s a driving force for keeping the modern student engaged.

Instead of a student procrastinating doing their hour of maths practise questions, they’re looking forward to doing an hour of GoMaths because they know that by the end, they’ll have (e.g.):
- overtaken their friend on their study group's leaderboard
- been rewarded with that cosmetic item that their character needs
- unlocked the next challenging level (difficulty)
- and practised their Maths (and automatically tracked that practise)!

With a firm design basis in behavioural psychology, we’re looking to really excel in this area and make sure that the motivation that a good private tutor gives is not lost in our software.


Accessibility:
GoMaths will be available on mobile and web, and designed to run smoothly even on low-cost devices. Furthermore, our business model is an inexpensive monthly subscription which will be significantly cheaper than even a single hour of private tuition. By doing so, we aim to make high-quality support accessible to all families.

Keeping our costs low will be a main concern, so we plan to take full advantage of the fact that we’re domain-specific; by (e.g.) intelligently caching learning pathways and generated content, our model costs can continuously be lowered, especially as we gain more users and extract more patterns and trends.


To summarise, our aim for GoMaths is to exceed the role of the traditional, human private tutor. So far, we’ve developed and released an MVP focused on the gamification aspects, and have begun developing our content banks. At the end of August 2025, we had our first paying customer.

ai_problem_solving:
AI helps us solve the problem of providing personalised, tutor-level support at scale to many students, regardless of location or household income. In alignment with Goal 4 of the Sustainable Development Goals, our aim is to make high-quality education universally accessible, removing barriers of cost and circumstance, and giving all students access to an AI teacher that’s tailored to teaching them.

Without AI, students predominantly rely on static revision tools (textbooks, pre-recorded videos, generic question banks) or expensive, hard-to-access private tutors. Static resources cannot adapt to a student’s unique misconceptions, motivation levels, or progress over time, leading to poor learning trajectories. Tutors can (if a good match with a student is made), but they are limited by cost, availability, and significant variability in quality.

Ultimately, AI addresses the critical challenge of students not receiving the personalised support they need to truly thrive; every learner benefits from explanations, challenges and walkthroughs that are tailored and adapt specifically to them, rather than a ‘one-size-fits-all’ alternative. In the past, the only way to access this degree of support has been through private tutors (and rarely, school support).

With advances in AI models (particularly conversational ones) we believe that we can now deliver the individualised, engaging support of a one-to-one tutor, available anytime a student needs it. Imagine a student stuck on a maths concept that just won’t click. Instead of staying frustrated or falling behind, their AI tutor identifies the best path forward, drawing on their past learning patterns and our unique model of their knowledge state. It adapts in real time to their micro-progress, providing guided practice, interactive examples/questions, and clear explanations, continuing until real understanding is achieved and validated.

By providing these tailored explanations, adaptive exercises, and encouragement that keeps learners motivated, AI makes it possible to deliver the human tutor-level support at a fraction of the cost, ensuring high-quality, personalised education is accessible to every student, regardless of background or income.

problem_description:
Universities face a growing challenge in assessing essays (1,000 to 20,000 words): providing clear, actionable feedback is essential for learning, yet remains one of the most time-consuming tasks academics undertake. The result is slower turnaround, variability in feedback, and lower student satisfaction.

This challenge is intensified by the rise of students using AI to compose their essays. Tools like ChatGPT produce writing that appears fluent but often lacks depth or coherence, leaving academics to spend additional time disentangling superficial prose in order to identify underlying weaknesses. Universities therefore need new ways to ensure fair and rigorous evaluation at scale.

The system, Marginalia, was developed at The Cultural Me in 2024/2025 to cope with demand for our publishing platform, where we help students edit and publish mainstream versions of their university essays. The timeliness and clarity of the new feedback has seen 100% improvement in writer retention. 

Marginalia strips away AI ‘padding’, giving academics a clearer view of the student’s real intentions. By surfacing structural, logical and thematic issues and suggesting draft comments for the reviewer to refine, Marginalia reduced evaluation times by 50% and doubled writer retention rates.

problem_impact:
In 2022-23, the UK had 240K academics (HESA). Collectively they evaluate an estimated 29 million essays each year, consuming millions of staff hours. For an individual lecturer, this typically means around 100 essays and 20 dissertations annually, with marking allocations of just one hour for a 3,000-word essay and three hours for a 10,000-word dissertation.

Within these tight limits, lecturers are expected to provide detailed and actionable feedback. The result is significant strain: marking becomes rushed, feedback quality suffers, and staff experience heightened stress. Students, in turn, receive comments that are often less detailed or constructive than necessary for their intellectual development.
customer_type: {"Lecturers and Researchers (Higher Education and Academic Research)"}
elevator_pitch: Marginalia is an AI tool that helps academics deliver fast, contextual feedback on complex essays with minimal effort — cutting evaluation time in half while keeping educators firmly in control. The tool provides a unique deconstructed insight into the student’s work.
solution_explanation:
Marginalia integrates directly into university LMSs via the LTI protocol, so lecturers can access it easily within their existing workflow. When an essay is submitted, it is processed through our AI pipeline.


The pipeline combines large language models, Python packages, and ML models to extract the text and decompose it into what we call Interpretive Blocks — a method developed through research at The Cultural Me. This decomposition allows long-form essays (up to 20,000 words) to be grasped by a human reviewer within minutes, while also exposing padding typical of AI-generated text.


Once decomposed, the essay is analysed by specialist modules that check for, among other things, thematic drift, logical flaws, and gaps in theoretical understanding. The results are collated and presented to the lecturer in a user-friendly interface.


The interface also provides draft feedback snippets linked to specific issues. Using a simple drag-and-drop feature, the academic can refine and synthesise these into final feedback.


Taken together, Marginalia helps academics quickly comprehend complex essays, identify weaknesses, and provide clear, contextual feedback with far less effort — all while keeping educators firmly in control of the evaluation.
program_goals:
By taking part in the programme, we hope to 

a) Learn how to gather clear evidence on whether Marginal saves time and improves the quality of feedback.

b) Work with UCL researchers to sharpen our methods for evaluating student writing and ensure our impact claims are robust.

c) Get practical guidance on pricing and procurement so our business model fits how universities actually buy tools like this.

d) Learn how to present Marginalia in a compelling way to both investors and university decision-makers.

e) Build lasting connections with other founders and education specialists who can challenge our thinking and strengthen our approach.
ai_problem_solving:
AI enables Marginalia to address challenges in essay evaluation that would be impossible or prohibitively time-consuming for human reviewers alone.

AI helps in the following ways:

a) Speed of processing: Large language models can now process a 10,000-word essay within a single context window. This allows the system to highlight relevant background facts, check the logical consistency of a student’s argument, and surface issues in seconds rather than hours.

b) Decomposition: AI can break down long, complex essays into their primary arguments and thematic blocks. This gives academics a clearer view of the student’s reasoning structure.

c) Feedback generation: AI can instantly draft feedback snippets related to specific problems in the essay. These are suggestions that the academic can synthesise to compose the final feedback, keeping the reviewer firmly in control while preserving their mental energy for higher-value teaching tasks.

In combination, these capabilities allow academics to solve the problem of providing fast, contextual feedback on complex essays with minimal effort. This could not be achieved without AI.

problem_impact:
For students aged 18–24 in higher education, the shortcomings in consent and harassment education have serious consequences. National surveys show that one in five students report experiencing unwanted sexual behaviour in a single academic year, with women more than twice as likely as men to be affected. These experiences erode students’ sense of safety and belonging, leaving many feeling unsafe in classrooms, residences, and social spaces.

The impact extends to mental health, with higher rates of anxiety, depression, and trauma among those affected. Students report difficulties with sleep, concentration, and confidence, which in turn undermines their academic performance. Some avoid classes, withdraw from social or extracurricular life, or even leave university altogether.

Trust in institutions is also weakened. Many students choose not to report harassment due to fear of stigma or a belief that nothing will change, reinforcing a cycle where harm continues unaddressed. The result is a generation of students navigating university life under conditions that compromise their wellbeing, learning, and long-term opportunities.

elevator_pitch: Arti delivers personalised, impactful, and trauma-informed consent training for university students through a conversation with an AI chatbot. It adapts to each student's needs and questions, integrates campus policies and culture, and scales to reach every student, every year.
solution_explanation:
Arti solves this by replacing passive modules with an interactive, one-to-one conversation. Instead of racing to the last slide, students engage in dialogue that challenges assumptions, checks understanding, and adapts content to their needs. This combines proven learning methods—feedback, retrieval practice, personalisation—while making the experience feel relevant and engaging.

Because Arti uses retrieval-augmented generation, it can answer students’ real questions and link directly to their university’s own policies, reporting tools, and resources. It also applies trauma-informed design, giving students control over triggers, offering empathetic support, and always signposting professional help.

Unlike static training, Arti stays fresh year after year. The system builds on prior knowledge, so returning students don’t face the same material twice but instead see new scenarios that grow with them.

Our Oxford randomized controlled trial confirmed the impact: Arti improved knowledge retention by 65%, was twice as effective at reducing negative attitudes, and 95% of participants wanted further training afterwards. Students described the experience as cathartic, empowering, and more human than “click-through” modules.
program_goals:
By taking part in this programme, we want to strengthen the foundations needed to scale Arti responsibly and successfully. Our top priority is to improve the technical delivery of pilots and refine our product so that it can truly impact how students learn about sexual harassment and consent. We want to ensure every stage of delivery is rigorous, effective, and backed by strong research.

We also want to validate our approach using structured research methods, so that Arti’s development remains evidence-based and grounded in measurable outcomes. Access to expertise in education technology and ethics will be invaluable in helping us achieve this without compromising impact.

Equally important, we would value the university connections that UCL Edtech labs can provide. Building relationships with institutional stakeholders is critical to accelerating pilots, gathering feedback, and ensuring adoption at scale. Finally, support in fundraising and storytelling will be key to helping us grow across the UK and, in time, internationally.

ai_problem_solving:
In short, AI solves the problem of delivering engaging, personalised, and scalable consent and harassment education that current methods cannot achieve. 

It can deliver adaptive, interactive learning that goes beyond the tick-box training, tailoring content to students’ contexts and responses at scale which human facilitators cannot feasibly provide to thousands of students. Students can ask their own questions, access university-specific policies and resources, and receive relevant, accurate guidance in real time. 

Crucially, AI also makes it possible to provide annual training without repetition fatigue. Content can evolve with each student’s prior knowledge and engagement, ensuring the learning remains fresh, meaningful, and effective throughout their university journey.

problem_description:
Education remains locked in one-size-fits-all delivery. Learners’ differing styles, paces, languages and accessibility needs are poorly served, driving disengagement and uneven attainment. Institutions struggle to personalise at scale. Existing platforms are fragmented and largely static, offering limited adaptivity and weak feedback loops, which depress outcomes and widen gaps.

Four pressures intensify the problem: (1) educator shortages and uneven standards; (2) escalating delivery costs; (3) curricula and skills needs that change faster than materials and methods; and (4) equity challenges for multilingual, neurodiverse and disadvantaged learners. 

The consequence is a persistent engagement and attainment gap, and rising institutional workload and risk.

solution_explanation:
ScholarMe fuses educational psychology with adaptive AI to address each structural constraint:
	•	Shortages & uneven standards. Provides consistent, high-quality tutoring irrespective of local staffing; adapts to each learner in real time, reducing dependence on variable teaching quality.
	•	Rising costs. Automates syllabus transformation into notes, quizzes, podcasts, videos—lowering the marginal cost of personalisation for institutions and families.
	•	Outdated materials & slow revision cycles. Ingests documents, lectures, video and audio (OCR/ASR), creating structured, up-to-date content libraries aligned to objectives and levels.
	•	Disengagement & inequity. Tracks engagement and behaviours; adjusts sequence, difficulty and modality to sustain motivation; avatar tutors humanise the experience; gamification sustains momentum; multilingual and accessibility features ensure inclusion.
	•	Assessment gap. Delivers instant, explanatory feedback; identifies knowledge gaps; recommends targeted remediation — closing the loop between learning and progress.
	•	Institutional visibility. Admin dashboards, cohort analytics and LTI-compliant integration support educator action and smooth adoption.
program_goals:
Clear, accelerator-driven progression from validated MVP to scalable market solution:
	1.	Product–market fit. 3–5 institutional pilots across education stages, with measured gains in engagement, retention, confidence and exam performance.
	2.	Go-to-market refinement. Pricing tests across B2B/B2C; positioning against competitors; alignment to institutional procurement.
	3.	Strategic partnerships. Distribution with LMS providers and edtech networks.
	4.	Governance & compliance. Strengthened data-privacy and safeguarding frameworks across jurisdictions.
	5.	Investor readiness. Investor-grade plan and financials for a £250k seed round post-programme, anchored in pilot evidence.

problem_description:
Across classrooms worldwide, 15–20% of children struggle with learning differences such as dyslexia, dyscalculia, ADHD, and autism. Schools face three persistent problems:
Early identification gaps — children are often diagnosed late, after years of academic failure, because teachers lack accessible, classroom-based tools.
Talent and resource shortages — there is a global shortfall of special educators and therapists; even in well-resourced schools, the ratio is often 1 specialist for hundreds of children.
Fragmented support systems — current interventions rely on pull-out models, are not scalable, and fail to equip mainstream teachers to manage diverse classrooms.
As a result, millions of children fall behind in literacy and numeracy, teachers experience burnout, and schools struggle to deliver inclusive education despite legal and policy mandates.
SpEd@school directly addresses this gap by enabling teachers to identify, support, and monitor children with special needs inside the classroom — reducing dependence on scarce specialists while ensuring measurable learning gains.

problem_impact:
Children in Grades K–6 with undetected or unsupported learning differences experience persistent gaps in literacy and numeracy at a foundational stage. Without timely identification and classroom support, these difficulties escalate into:
Academic underachievement — children fall behind peers in core subjects, often by 1–2 grade levels.
Reduced confidence and motivation — repeated failure erodes self-esteem and engagement, leading to school avoidance.
Increased behavioural challenges — frustration in the classroom can manifest as inattention, withdrawal, or disruptive behaviour.
Widening inequity — children from under-resourced schools or regions without access to specialists are disproportionately affected.
By the time these gaps are formally addressed, many children have already missed critical developmental windows, making intervention more intensive, expensive, and less effective.

elevator_pitch: SpEd@school equips teachers to identify and support learning differences in-class, using AI for early screening, personalised interventions, and progress tracking—scaling inclusive education and improving literacy/numeracy outcomes globally.
solution_explanation:
SpEd@school bridges the gap between the growing number of children with learning differences and the limited capacity of schools to identify and support them. Our solution works at three levels:
Early Identification – Using AI-powered tools like SpEdProbe and SpEdJunior, teachers can screen children inside the classroom. These assessments analyse performance and error patterns, generating immediate reports with risk flags and recommended strategies. This reduces reliance on scarce specialists and ensures issues are caught years earlier than traditional pathways.
Classroom-Based Interventions – Instead of pull-out therapy models, our platform provides teachers with curriculum-aligned, tiered adaptations that can be applied during regular lessons. Each child’s Individualised Education Plan (IEP) is auto-generated and includes evidence-backed strategies categorised by mild, moderate, or severe need.
Continuous Monitoring & Support – Progress is tracked through digital dashboards, allowing teachers and parents to see gains in literacy, numeracy, and cognitive skills. AI models reduce teacher workload by automating data capture and providing actionable insights, while gamified tools like SpEdPLAY keep children engaged.
By integrating assessment, intervention, and monitoring into a single platform, SpEd@school empowers mainstream teachers to deliver inclusion at scale, improves academic outcomes, and reduces systemic dependency on specialist resources.
program_goals:
SpEd@school addresses the global gap between children with learning differences and the limited resources available to support them by integrating assessment, intervention, and monitoring within the classroom:
Early Identification – AI-powered tools (SpEdProbe, SpEdJunior) screen students in under 20 minutes, generating automatic reports with risk flags and classroom strategies. In pilots, this reduced screening time by 60% and improved early detection accuracy by 25% compared to teacher-only identification.
Classroom-Based Interventions – Auto-generated Individualised Education Plans (IEPs) provide teachers with curriculum-aligned strategies tailored to mild, moderate, or severe needs. In 12 pilot schools, children showed an average 18% improvement in reading fluency and 22% in numeracy accuracy over a single term.
Continuous Monitoring & Support – Dashboards track progress and automate reporting, cutting teacher workload on progress tracking by 35%. Gamified modules (SpEdPLAY) further improve engagement, with students completing 40% more practice tasks compared to traditional worksheets.
By equipping mainstream teachers with scalable, evidence-backed tools, SpEd@school ensures earlier support, measurable learning gains, and sustainable inclusion without dependence on scarce specialists.

ai_problem_solving:
AI enables us to address challenges in identification, personalisation, and scale that are impossible through human-only methods. Specifically:
Early Identification at Scale – Teachers cannot reliably screen every child for subtle patterns of learning difficulties. Our AI models analyse performance, error patterns, and engagement data to flag risks earlier and more accurately.
Personalisation in Real Time – Human teachers cannot adapt lesson content dynamically for 30+ students at once. AI personalises literacy and numeracy pathways, adjusting difficulty and sequencing as each child progresses.
Reducing Subjectivity – Teacher judgements of learning differences can be inconsistent. AI standardises assessment and recommendations, ensuring fairness and inclusivity.
Continuous Monitoring – AI automates progress tracking, providing teachers with actionable insights without adding to workload.
In our pilots, AI reduced teacher screening time by 60% and improved early detection accuracy by 25%, ensuring that more children received timely support while teachers focused on instruction.
ai_team_expertise:
Our AI expertise is built on a blend of clinical leadership, educational insight, institutional partnerships, and an in-house tech team:
Founder & CEO (Dr. Dhaval Mody) – over 30 years in child and adolescent psychiatry, with extensive experience in learning disabilities and digital interventions, ensuring AI development addresses genuine classroom and clinical needs.
Clinical & Educational Advisors – senior special educators and psychologists guide us to design AI systems that are pedagogically sound, ethically responsible, and aligned with inclusive education practices.
Institutional Partnerships – collaborations with universities and research institutions support product validation, provide access to data frameworks, and strengthen the rigor of our AI-driven models.
In-house Tech Team – a dedicated group of developers and data engineers capable of building and deploying AI models within the SPEED LDS platform, ensuring integration and scalability.
While we have successfully developed and deployed AI-powered assessments (SpEdProbe, SpEdJunior) and gamified interventions (SpEdPLAY), we recognise the need to deepen our AI/ML research capacity. Through UCL EdTech Labs, we seek mentorship, methodological guidance, and academic partnerships to strengthen explainability, bias reduction, and large-scale validation of our models.

elevator_pitch: Most companies want to do AI, but up-skilling is generic and disconnected from workforce needs. Tangible makes learning verifiable and hiring precise with role based AI pathways and proof of work that link skills directly to jobs and mobility.
solution_explanation:
Problem: AI up-skilling is generic, hard to verify, and disconnected from hiring/internal mobility.

Solution (what we are building): Infrastructure for verifiable, role specific learning that produces proof of work and connects directly to precision hiring and redeployment.

Step-by-step (user journey)

Employer defines the role & outcomes
L&D/Hiring picks a target role and the few competencies that matter for the job (your role→competency map).

1. SMEs create a problem bank with AI: Subject matter experts turn real work into teachable problems (briefs, tickets, datasets). AI helps generate role/industry variants and simple rubrics.

2. Build a problem-based pathway: Problems are sequenced from starter → job-ready with short checklists and expected deliverables for each competency.

3. Learners do problem sprints (produce proof of work): Professionals complete problems and submit artifacts (docs, code, reports, workflows). Each submission is tied to a specific competency.

4. Subject Matter Expert (SME) verification: Reviewers and AI systems evaluate artifacts against the rubric (meets / almost / redo) with brief feedback. Competency decisions are recorded.

5. Verifiable skill record: The platform compiles passed competencies with links to the underlying artifacts into a shareable, evidence-backed record (stronger than a certificate).

6. Precision hiring & internal mobility: Hiring/HR can search by verified competencies and open the candidate’s proof of work directly, creating shortlists grounded in evidence.

Why this works
1. Role true, not generic: Paths are built from actual problems tied to the job.
2. Evidence, not guesses: Proof of work and SME decisions make skills verifiable.

problem_description:
Despite achieving gender parity in education, women in Latin America remain locked out of meaningful economic opportunities. Only 52% participate in the formal labor force compared to 76% of men (WEF). Many discover too late that their degrees fail to translate into stable, well-paying jobs: “My parents financed my studies hoping a degree would open doors. It didn’t. The careers I chose weren’t valued economically, and I faced a labor market where long hours didn’t even cover the basics.”
This exclusion is driven by interconnected systemic barriers. Affordable, high-quality upskilling that truly responds to market demand remains out of reach for most women. Existing training models are often either prohibitively expensive, overly generic, or designed for more privileged populations. As a result, they fail to account for the realities of women who are balancing unpaid care work, limited time, and financial precarity. Without access to flexible, relevant, and affordable education that bridges directly to labor market opportunities, women are left unprepared for the growing number of roles in the digital economy. 
Meanwhile, as AI and automation rapidly transform work, women—overrepresented in clerical and administrative positions—are nearly three times more likely than men to see their jobs automated (ILO, 2025). Yet those most at risk often lack the resources or support systems to adapt. 
The burden of unpaid care work further compounds this challenge. Women in the region spend three times more time than men on domestic responsibilities (UNDP, 2024), leaving little space for reskilling or career growth. As one shared: “I want to advance, but my child needs constant care. Even remote jobs require experience I don’t have.”
Adding to these structural barriers is the absence of professional networks, mentors, and role models. Social capital is one of the strongest predictors of upward mobility (Nature, 2022), yet women from low-income backgrounds are often excluded from these circles. This lack of connection deepens inequality, erodes confidence, and stalls careers.
To effectively unlock women’s potential, solutions must address not just skills gaps but also time, networks, and belief in what’s possible.

problem_impact:
Throughout Latin America, over 60% of women remain outside the formal economy, with 48 million either unemployed or working in vulnerable, underemployed conditions. Within this broader group, our target users are women living in urban areas and major financial capitals, aged 25 to 35, who have often pursued higher education yet struggle to access quality employment. 
Nearly 90% of participants are unemployed and are seeking economic stability. In their most recent jobs, they were earning as little as $400 per month—amounts that barely stretch to cover housing, healthcare, and food, leaving no room for career investment. Their most common jobs include roles as administrative staff, coordinators, or graphic designers. However, many have experienced challenging and exploitative work conditions. They frequently work in precarious positions without formal contracts or remain stuck in the same role for extended periods. Many have faced significant gender discrimination in the workplace, and in some cases have experienced hostile or violent behavior. These work conditions have prevented them from advancing professionally, contributing effectively, or developing their skills. They are also disproportionately affected by shifts in the labor market. Roles where women are overrepresented, are among the first to be displaced by automation and AI. Yet the training opportunities that could help them adapt are frequently inaccessible, unaffordable, or misaligned with market needs.
Socially, these women tend to be pioneers in their families—the first to attend university—which places a heavy burden of expectation on them to achieve social and economic mobility. Despite this, they often lack professional networks or role models to guide their career paths. Their personal lives are similarly demanding; most carry the full weight of household responsibilities and rely entirely on their familial support systems. 
Ultimately, these women are seeking stable, dignified employment with benefits and inclusive workplace cultures. They represent a massive and underserved market—larger than the entire population of Spain—with immense potential for economic impact if given the right tools, support, and opportunities. This population is resilient, ambitious, and eager to grow—but underserved by systems that were not designed with their realities in mind.

elevator_pitch: Laboratoria empowers women in Latin America by equipping participants with the tools, skills, and networks needed to overcome systemic exclusion from economic opportunity. Our goal is to transform lives and ensure women are included in the digital economy.
solution_explanation:
Our product provides a unique, integrated solution that addresses the multifaceted problem of systemic exclusion. We don't just offer skills training; we've built a complete career ecosystem designed to solve the interconnected challenges women face—from a lack of confidence and technical skills to the absence of professional networks. Our platform is a long-term, community-powered solution that transforms a participant's entire journey from job seeker to career leader.
1) Activa tu Carrera: Fixing Confidence and Mindset
The first and most critical problem our platform solves is the personal and psychological barrier that prevents women from pursuing opportunities. Many talented women lack the confidence and a clear roadmap to navigate the labor market, often due to their exclusion from supportive networks. Our Activa tu Carrera program directly addresses this through a transformative, cohort-based experience that helps women build a foundational mindset for success. Through professional coaching and structured networking, participants develop self-confidence, clarify their career goals, and create a personalized roadmap. This initial stage ensures they feel they belong and are not alone, directly combating the isolation and self-doubt that previously held them back.
2) Job Search Hub: Bridging Skills and Networks
Our platform solves the dual problem of inaccessible upskilling and a lack of professional networks. While other programs offer fragmented, high-cost training or low-engagement MOOCs, our Job Search Hub provides essential, market-aligned skills training—such as AI fundamentals and data analysis—within a community-driven space. This hub strengthens individual agency through collective momentum. Participants access practical tools like a Collaborative Job Board and receive personalized support from HR mentors. The core of this stage is our community, where weekly peer-to-peer sessions foster accountability and solidarity, ensuring that no woman navigates her job search in isolation but as part of a dynamic, connected network.
3) Ruta de Crecimiento: Building a Lifelong Community
The most unique aspect of our solution is our commitment to long-term support, which is essential for building sustained social capital. While many solutions stop at job placement, our Ruta de Crecimiento is a long-term ecosystem that empowers graduates to continue growing after landing their first job. This component directly solves the problem of stagnant career progression and limited networks by providing access to continuous learning, coaching, and mentorship. Through a Tech Skills Academy and community-led events, women strengthen their connections and share opportunities, reinforcing the power of their network as a catalyst for continued success and ensuring their journey is supported long after the initial program ends.
program_goals:
By participating in the UCL EdTech Labs program, our primary goal is to gain expert guidance and strategic support to tackle the unique scalability challenges of our community-powered career acceleration platform. After a decade of proven success equipping women from low-income communities with digital skills, we are at a pivotal moment, aiming to reach 100,000 women across Latin America by 2030. Achieving this requires us to scale our personalized, human-centric model without losing the quality and community that define us. 
This is why we have built our new community-driven platform. The platform will reduce costs, increase efficiency, and ensure sustainable, scalable growth, empowering more women with the tools and resources they need to achieve upward mobility and economic opportunity.
We believe UCL's expertise in educational technology is essential for this next phase of our growth. 
1) Refining Our Model Through Research
As we move to a broader, more scalable platform, we need to pressure-test our new core offerings, such as "Activa tu Carrera" and "Job Search Hub", and gain a sharper insight of our users. We would leverage UCL's structured research methods to validate our assumptions and build an evidence-based framework that ensures our new platform maintains the intimacy and trust our original cohorts experienced. By mastering these research methods, we can refine our integrated learning pathways to deliver meaningful outcomes at a significantly larger scale.
2) Deploying AI to Amplify Human Connection
A core component of our new model is leveraging AI to provide a personalized, high-quality experience to thousands of women simultaneously. We are not using AI to replace human interaction but to augment it, a shift we see as crucial for our ambitious goals. We hope to work with UCL's experts in the AIEd Stream to move from our current understanding to a concrete AI implementation plan. Our goal is to deploy what matters—using AI to automate tasks like personalized mentor matching, skills recommendations and feedback—thereby freeing our coaches to focus on the human elements of confidence, community, and mentorship that make our model truly transformative. We want to ensure that as our platform grows, our AI maintains the equity-centered approach that underpins our mission, while effectively reducing the need for one-on-one human support.
3) Building a Narrative for Scalable Impact
Ultimately, we want to translate our validated model and AI strategy into a compelling narrative that can help us achieve our mission. By the end of the program, we hope to have a polished, philanthropic investor-ready pitch deck that demonstrates not only our historical success but also our strategic, scalable, and data-driven path forward. By showcasing our ability to maintain high quality and personalized support at a massive scale, we can attract the funding necessary to truly anchor our platform as the leading career acceleration platform for women in the region.

ai_problem_solving:
The primary problem that AI uniquely helps us solve is scalability without sacrificing personalization. In our mission to empower women from underserved backgrounds, building trust and social capital is critical. This is fundamentally a human-centric process, yet our goal is to reach 100,000 women across Latin America by 2030. Without AI, it would be impossible to scale our model and provide the kind of personalized support and community-building that our participants need to overcome systemic barriers.
AI addresses this specific educational problem in four key ways:
1. Scaling Human-like Support:
We know that trust and confidence are built through human interaction. AI doesn't replace this; instead, it provides human augmentation, allowing our staff to be more efficient and impactful. For example, AI can analyze a participant’s skills and goals to make highly tailored recommendations for mentors, relevant events, and learning circles. By automating these matching processes, we free up our human coaches to focus on the high-touch, empathetic interactions that truly build trust and confidence. This allows us to serve more women per staff member without compromising on the quality of support.
2. Personalizing the Career Journey at Scale:
The career journey is not linear, and each participant has unique needs. AI helps us create a personalized experience for thousands of women simultaneously, which could not be done manually. It can analyze data from a participant’s progress, their professional profile, and their engagement on the platform to offer customized suggestions for skills to acquire or jobs to apply for. This level of personalized guidance helps women navigate a complex labor market, addressing their specific needs and helping them move from career activation to job search to professional development smoothly and efficiently.
3. Fostering Community and Social Capital:
A core part of our mission is to build social capital. AI is a powerful tool for facilitating these connections at scale. It can suggest relevant peer-to-peer groups and learning circles based on shared interests or career goals, ensuring that every participant feels a sense of belonging. By leveraging AI to make these connections, we can build and sustain engagement, creating a dynamic, connected network that is essential for their long-term success.
4. AI is at the Core of What We Teach:
AI is not only a tool for how we operate; it's a fundamental part of what we teach. The digital economy is rapidly shifting, and AI is at the forefront of this transformation. We incorporate AI skills accelerators and data fundamentals training into our curriculum to prepare women for the jobs of tomorrow. By teaching AI skills we are helping them enter an emerging field that offers significant opportunities for growth. This dual approach, using AI to scale our support while also making it a central part of our curriculum, ensures our solution is both innovative and market-aligned.

At Bubbled we aim to solve these market inefficiencies (e.g., affordability and trust) and customer pain points (e.g., complexity and information asymmetry) with a new model: An AI-native and peer-driven admissions marketplace and content platform for university students, where successful alumni act as freelance admissions coaches and content creators, offering high-quality and high-trust counsel, content, and tooling at competitive rates. We aim to be AI-native by embedding AI into core solutions (AI-powered search, analytics, tooling) and operations (e.g., product, design, engineering, and growth) from day 1.

elevator_pitch: SymphoMe is an AI-powered music education platform for learners and teachers. It delivers real-time feedback and smart practice tools, reducing dropout and boosting progress. Unlike traditional lessons or apps, SymphoMe extends learning beyond the classroom.
solution_explanation:
SymphoMe solves this problem by acting as a 24/7 music tutor that supports learners during their independent practice. Our AI listens to performances in real time, detecting errors in pitch, rhythm, and expression, and then provides immediate, personalised feedback, just as a human teacher would. This keeps learners motivated, confident, and progressing between lessons, dramatically reducing frustration and dropout rates.

At the same time, SymphoMe integrates teachers into the process. Through progress tracking and performance reports, teachers can see exactly where students struggled or improved outside lesson time. This allows them to use class time more effectively and extend their impact beyond the classroom.

Unlike existing solutions that either offer static video tutorials or isolated gamified practice, SymphoMe combines real-time feedback, adaptive learning, and teacher integration into a single platform. This dual approach not only protects the learner’s investment in music education but also helps teachers capture long-term value that is otherwise lost when students quit early.
program_goals: By joining the UCL EdTech Accelerator, I aim to strengthen SymphoMe’s growth strategy as we scale our live product, already used by paying customers. With a £750,000 pre-seed round currently open and 21% committed, the programme would help us refine our go-to-market approach, expand school and B2C adoption, and connect with the right mentors, partners, and investors to position SymphoMe as a global leader in AI-powered music education.
ai_problem_solving: SymphoMe AI is a patent-pending technology that is capable of offering context-aware feedback to music students like no other solution on the market today, supporting students by up to 80% of their progression and enabling music teachers to support their students within and in-between lessons. Although our first solution was built for piano, we have developed a thorough understanding of what doesn't work in this space, that we can confidently unlock other instruments through the next funding round or/and over the next 6 - 12 months.

elevator_pitch: Another Set of Eyes is building a universal smart assistant that captures expert knowledge with AI and AR, and delivers real-time guidance to make work safer and smarter at scale.
solution_explanation:
Our product solves the challenge of capturing, retaining, and transferring tacit knowledge — the kind of expertise that is hard to document but essential for safety, productivity, and business continuity. Traditional manuals, videos, or classroom training cannot replicate how experts actually see and act in real-world environments, especially under pressure.

How do we solve this:

Capture tacit knowledge: With smart glasses and eye-tracking, we capture exactly how top performers scan environments, detect risks, and make decisions.

Model expertise with AI: AI then classifies and structures this knowledge, learning the best practices that are otherwise difficult to articulate.

Deliver adaptive guidance: Our AR- and mobile-first platform transforms this into real-time, hands-free guidance tailored to each worker’s context, accelerating learning and reducing errors.

Hardware-independent design: Unlike other solutions tied to a single vendor, our platform is hardware-agnostic. Clients can “plug and play” with their device of choice — whether that’s different smart glasses, mobile phones, or tablets — ensuring flexibility, scalability, and future-proofing as the market evolves.

Continuous improvement: Each deployment strengthens the models, enabling organisations to refine training and performance support over time.

This approach allows companies to upskill and reskill workers faster, reduce safety incidents, and retain critical expertise — all while maintaining freedom of choice in their hardware strategy.
program_goals: By participating in this programme, we aim to refine our universal smart assistant for workforce learning so it delivers evidence-based, effective, and scalable training. We want to validate our AI-driven, eye-tracking, and AR approach against academic research, iterate based on real-world educator and learner feedback, and accelerate adoption in enterprise and industrial learning environments. Ultimately, our goal is to transform how tacit knowledge and complex skills are captured, taught, and retained, creating measurable improvements in learning outcomes, safety, and workplace performance at scale.
ai_problem_solving: Our eye-tracking and smart glasses technology captures what experts actually see and do, but it’s AI that makes this knowledge actionable. By designing the right models, we can learn patterns of best practice that are almost impossible to write down or teach through manuals — the tacit knowledge that defines true expertise. AI classifies, structures, and contextualises this data, turning it into adaptive, real-time guidance for workers. elevator_pitch: Skeptiq is a safety and critical thinking tool for AI. It detects emotional distress, over-reliance, and unsafe therapy use, checks the reliability of sources, and prompts reflection. Skeptiq helps students use AI safely, critically, and with confidence.
solution_explanation:
Skeptiq directly addresses the risks students face when using AI — unsafe therapy use, over-reliance, and misinformation, by combining real-time monitoring with critical thinking support.

Emotional Safety
Skeptiq detects emotional language such as sadness, hopelessness, or anxiety. When concerning patterns appear, the tool flags them, offers supportive nudges, and, in severe cases, can signpost students to appropriate support services. This prevents AI from reinforcing harmful emotional states or encouraging unsafe behaviour.

Over-Reliance and Excessive Use
The tool monitors when students delegate too many decisions to AI, show dependency signals (“I don’t want to think about this”), or use the system excessively. It then provides gentle warnings and prompts to encourage healthier, more independent study habits.

Trigger Word and Sensitive Information Detection
Skeptiq flags dangerous or high-risk keywords, including those linked to self-harm, suicide, or plagiarism. It also detects when students try to share sensitive personal information, such as student IDs, emails, or phone numbers, and issues protective warnings to prevent data misuse.

Source and Intent Check
Skeptiq checks whether references and citations provided by AI actually exist. This helps students avoid relying on fabricated, biased or misleading sources and ensures their academic work is grounded in real, verifiable information.

Critical Thinking Support
Through the Skeptiq Script, students receive rotating smart questions designed to challenge both their prompts and the AI’s responses. This builds reflection into the learning process, helping students think more deeply instead of passively absorbing outputs.

The Friction Score
All of these interactions feed into a “Friction Score,” which measures how actively students are engaging with AI. A higher score reflects safer, more critical use. This creates a tangible metric for responsible AI engagement, which can be fed back to students or educators.

By combining emotional safeguards, trigger word detection, sensitive information protection, source checking, and reflective questioning, Skeptiq transforms AI from a potential risk into a supportive educational partner; protecting student well-being while strengthening academic integrity and digital literacy.
program_goals:
By joining this programme, we aim to accelerate the development of Skeptiq into a scalable AI safety and critical thinking tool for students. Specifically, we want to:

Refine and expand our safety and thought modules with guidance from UCL experts in AI and education.

Pilot Skeptiq within real learning environments to gather user feedback and robust data on student AI use.

Build research partnerships that strengthen the academic evidence base around safe, responsible AI use in education.

Access mentorship, networks, and resources that will help us shape Skeptiq into a globally deployable solution, starting with UK universities.

Our ultimate goal is to position Skeptiq as the standard safeguard for student AI use; protecting well-being, strengthening academic integrity, and preparing students for a digital future.

problem_description:
Frontline pharmaceutical sales representatives in Latin America face a triple challenge:
Low knowledge retention & engagement: Up to 70% of training is forgotten within 30 days without reinforcement (Ebbinghaus forgetting curve; Murre & Dros, 2015). Training is often delivered via lengthy Zoom sessions, where reps are distracted, their cameras are off, and engagement is low.
Costly errors & compliance risks: Mis-entered orders, wrong SKUs, or incorrect discounts lead to cancelled invoices, lost sales, and regulatory exposure. In regulated sectors, such as pharmaceuticals, inadequate compliance training can result in legal penalties.
High turnover & lack of scalability: Recent labor law changes (e.g., Bolivia eliminating probation periods) mean reps are permanent employees from day one. Companies need them to be productive fast, but have limited L&D resources and no time for repeated in-person training.
These gaps create a cycle of inefficiency, characterized by slow onboarding, repeated mistakes, lower sales performance, and higher training costs.
Our product addresses this by delivering bite-sized, mobile-first, Teams- and WhatsApp-native microlearning, with daily nudges and quizzes that enhance retention, reduce errors, and provide managers with real-time visibility into who is field-ready. This is a localized, regulatory-compliant, and scalable solution purpose-built for the unique realities of the pharmaceutical sector in LATAM.
References: 
Murre, J. M. J., & Dros, J. (2015). Replication and Analysis of Ebbinghaus’ Forgetting Curve. PLOS ONE, 10(7), e0120644. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0120644. Wimmer, M. C., Perner, J., & Kirk, E. (2018). The impact of repetition on memory and retention in training: Evidence from applied learning contexts. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition, 44(8), 1251–1265. https://doi.org/10.1037/xlm0000496

problem_impact:
As a digital transformation consultant working with education, pharmaceutical, and healthcare companies in Latin America, I have personally witnessed how inadequate training hinders frontline teams, leads to repeated errors, and frustrates managers. The impact is significant:
High turnover & lost investment: Pharma sales turnover is ~35%, with 44% of representatives leaving within 1–2 years, long before they reach full productivity. Replacing a single sales representative costs 1–2× their annual salary when you include recruitment, onboarding, and lost sales.
Slow ramp-up: In similar B2B contexts, reps need 3–5 months to reach full productivity. Without reinforcement, up to 70% of training is forgotten within 30 days (Ebbinghaus forgetting curve). This forces managers to repeat training, pulling time away from revenue-generating activities.
Operational errors & compliance risk: I have seen errors in order entry, SKU selection, and discount application lead to cancelled invoices and inventory mismatches, real financial loss and reputational damage. In regulated sectors like pharmaceuticals, overlooked compliance protocols can lead to legal exposure.

elevator_pitch: We’re creating the first LATAM-specific, pharma microlearning platform that uses AI to convert SOPs into structured, evidence-based lessons on WhatsApp and Teams. Our human-in-the-loop approach ensures compliance, measurable retention, and tangible impact in the real world.
solution_explanation:
Our platform solves the problem by transforming slow, one-size-fits-all training into continuous, personalised learning delivered through the channels LATAM pharmaceutical reps already use, such as WhatsApp and Teams.
First, our AI engine ingests compliance-heavy SOPs, product manuals, and training PDFs, converting them into structured micro-lessons and quizzes in minutes. Each lesson is automatically tagged by role and version-controlled in Postgres, ensuring regulatory traceability.
Second, our event-driven workflow enables instant regeneration whenever an SOP or regulation changes, replacing what used to take weeks of human redesign with a single automated step. This keeps training always current and reduces compliance risk.
Third, we apply spaced repetition and daily nudges, proven to improve retention compared to traditional learning, and provide managers with real-time dashboards showing which reps are field-ready and where gaps remain.
Finally, we include human-in-the-loop review before publishing to guarantee accuracy and contextual relevance, combining the scale of AI with the accountability of expert oversight.
This approach reduces onboarding time by 40% (internal pilot), lowers error rates in order entry and compliance processes, and improves rep engagement, breaking the cycle of slow ramp-up and repeated mistakes that currently cost LATAM pharmaceutical companies time, sales, and regulatory exposure.

ai_problem_solving:
AI enables us to transform compliance-heavy pharmaceutical training content, such as PDFs, SOPs, and product manuals, into structured, editable micro-lessons in minutes, at a scale and speed impossible for human-only teams.
Traditionally, companies rely on instructional designers to manually extract key points, write learning objectives, create quizzes, and update materials whenever regulations or product data change. This process takes weeks, is costly, and is out of reach for many Latin American pharmaceutical companies that have no dedicated teams.
Our MVP combines LLM-powered parsing and summarization to convert PDFs into Markdown and automatically generate structured lesson JSON. It features event-driven lesson generation workers that detect new documents, build lessons, and store them with full version control in PostgreSQL. A human-in-the-loop review step ensures compliance accuracy and contextual relevance before distribution, and instant update capabilities allow all affected lessons to be regenerated automatically whenever SOPs or regulations change, keeping training continuously up to date.
In our next iteration, we are integrating Model Context Protocol (MCP) to make lesson generation safer, more reliable, and model-agnostic, enabling us to swap LLM providers, fine-tune models, and regenerate lessons at scale without re-engineering the pipeline.

problem_description:
The problem we are addressing is the lack of structured support for talented school students who aim to compete in national and international Olympiads in mathematics, physics, and related subjects.

Despite exceptional raw talent, many students lack access to experienced mentors, high-quality training resources, and a supportive peer community. Participation is often dependent on whether a student happens to have a motivated teacher at their school — meaning success is tied more to luck than potential. This results in:
	•	Underrepresentation of capable students at national and international competitions.
	•	Missed opportunities for students to develop advanced problem-solving skills critical for STEM careers.
	•	Burnout and attrition among teachers who support students without systemic resources or recognition.

By failing to provide a structured pathway, the current system leaves brilliant young minds unsupported, limiting both individual futures and the broader STEM talent pipeline.

problem_impact:
For students in grades 7–12, the absence of structured Olympiad preparation creates significant barriers at a critical stage of their development. Many talented students:
	•	Miss out on opportunities to participate in national or international Olympiads simply because their schools lack qualified mentors or advanced resources.
	•	Face uneven access — success often depends on geography or whether a student happens to have a teacher willing to train them beyond the curriculum.
	•	Lose motivation when their talent is not recognised or supported, leading to disengagement from STEM subjects just as they are making key decisions about future studies.
	•	Struggle with isolation — without peers or communities who share their interests, many gifted students lack the encouragement to persevere.
	•	Never discover their potential passion — since the school maths curriculum is very different from Olympiad-style problems, many students don’t even realise they have a gift or passion for the subject until they encounter a challenge that truly inspires them.

As a result, many capable students either underperform relative to their potential or drop out of the Olympiad track entirely. This not only narrows their individual opportunities for scholarships and STEM careers but also contributes to the underdevelopment of the national and international STEM talent pipeline.

solution_explanation:
ApexTalent solves the Olympiad preparation gap by creating a structured, scalable pathway for talented students in grades 7–12. The platform combines AI-driven personalisation with teacher-led guidance to ensure every student can access the right level of challenge, no matter their school or location.

AI analyses a student’s problem-solving approach and adapts tasks to their current level, keeping them engaged and motivated while preventing frustration. It also provides instant feedback and hints, reducing the dependency on scarce expert mentors. For teachers, the platform offers curated banks of Olympiad-style problems, performance dashboards, and ready-made training resources, easing their workload and expanding their reach.

By uniting adaptive learning, scalable mentorship, and community-building, ApexTalent ensures that students who might never encounter Olympiad-style problems in school can discover their talent, nurture it, and progress toward national and international competitions. This approach not only supports individual student growth but also strengthens the broader STEM talent pipeline.

I hope to gain access to UCL’s expertise in evidence-based EdTech design, refine our AI-driven personalisation, and test early pilots with a supportive academic network.

Beyond validation, I’d like to connect with mentors, potential collaborators, and funders who share a vision for making advanced STEM opportunities more accessible. research_evidence:
Title: How Students Use AI Feedback Matters: Experimental Evidence on Physics Achievement and Autonomy
Authors: Xusheng Dai, Zhaochun Wen, Jianxiao Jiang, Huiqin Liu, Yu Zhang
Date: May 2025 (arXiv preprint)
Abstract / Key Findings: In two randomized controlled trials with high-school physics students (N = 387 over 5 weeks), the study finds that AI-generated hint-style feedback significantly improves performance for lower-achieving students. For higher-achieving students, giving them autonomy in using feedback was more effective. The results highlight that how feedback is delivered (usage patterns) matters a lot.  
https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.08672
ai_problem_solving:
